metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Leisure, Digitalisering, Wet- en regelgeving
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: This article explains how the Digital Services Act will make ‘tracking’ individuals for advertising purposes more difficult and lists alternatives.
metadata.parent_text:
![Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/advertentiemodel-onder-druk-door-nieuwe-europese%20wetgeving_1800_1156511128_tcm16-158085.jpg)
Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation. [Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html). [Technology, Media & Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/index.html). 28/06/2022 • 10 minutes read. [Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/julia-krauwer/index.html). [Mario Bersem](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/mario-bersem/index.html). With the Digital Services Act, the days when personal data could be freely collected and traded for advertising purposes seem to be over. Although this new European legislation is primarily aimed at large tech companies such as Google and Facebook, smaller parties in the online advertising chain will also need to adjust their strategy. On 23 April, the Council of the European Union reached agreement on the Digital Services Act (DSA), a new set of rules intended to create a safe online environment for both citizens and businesses. Among other things, the DSA will make it harder to track internet users for advertising purposes. The revenues that websites now generate by offering space for personalised ads will no longer be a given. This may affect many parties; almost half of the most-visited websites earn money through advertising. The new law also means a switch for advertisers; in 2021, almost three-quarters of online advertising spend went to personalised ads. It is therefore important for both ‘publishers’, in advertising terms, and advertisers to try alternative advertising strategies. Contextual advertising can help, as can smart use of less privacy-sensitive data. Legislation for a safer internet. With the Digital Services Act and the ‘sister legislation’ Digital Markets Act (DMA), which was adopted earlier, the European Union is taking the lead in regulating the digital market, protecting personal data and curbing the power of large technology companies.

metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: This article explains how the Digital Services Act will make ‘tracking’ individuals for advertising purposes more difficult and lists alternatives.
metadata.parent_text:
![Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/media/advertentiemodel-onder-druk-door-nieuwe-europese%20wetgeving_1800_1156511128_tcm16-158085.jpg)
Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation. [Analysis](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/analyses/index.html). [Technology, Media & Telecom](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/sectoren-en-trends/technologie/index.html). 28/06/2022 • 10 minutes read. [Julia Krauwer](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/julia-krauwer/index.html). [Mario Bersem](https://www.abnamro.nl/nl/zakelijk/insights/auteurs/mario-bersem/index.html). With the Digital Services Act, the days when personal data could be freely collected and traded for advertising purposes seem to be over. Although this new European legislation is primarily aimed at large tech companies such as Google and Facebook, smaller parties in the online advertising chain will also need to adjust their strategy. On 23 April, the Council of the European Union reached agreement on the Digital Services Act (DSA), a new set of rules intended to create a safe online environment for both citizens and businesses. Among other things, the DSA will make it harder to track internet users for advertising purposes. The revenues that websites now generate by offering space for personalised ads will no longer be a given. This may affect many parties; almost half of the most-visited websites earn money through advertising. The new law also means a switch for advertisers; in 2021, almost three-quarters of online advertising spend went to personalised ads. It is therefore important for both ‘publishers’, in advertising terms, and advertisers to try alternative advertising strategies. Contextual advertising can help, as can smart use of less privacy-sensitive data. Legislation for a safer internet. With the Digital Services Act and the ‘sister legislation’ Digital Markets Act (DMA), which was adopted earlier, the European Union is taking the lead in regulating the digital market, protecting personal data and curbing the power of large technology companies.

id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0013
text: The DSA addresses two major challenges in today’s digital world: the fact that online platforms are being misused to distribute illegal products, disinformation and hate speech; and the overall lack of transparency in, and oversight of, data use by digital service providers. Given this second challenge, it is not surprising that the DSA also includes various rules relating to online advertising.

metadata.chunk_id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0013
metadata.source_slug: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving
metadata.source_title: Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Leisure, Digitalisering, Wet- en regelgeving
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: This article explains how the Digital Services Act will make ‘tracking’ individuals for advertising purposes more difficult and lists alternatives.
metadata.parent_text: The DSA addresses two major challenges in today’s digital world: the fact that online platforms are being misused to distribute illegal products, disinformation and hate speech; and the overall lack of transparency in, and oversight of, data use by digital service providers. Given this second challenge, it is not surprising that the DSA also includes various rules relating to online advertising. Data are an essential raw material for many digital advertising campaigns. According to the ‘Digital Ad Spend Study’, an annual report by consultant Deloitte and industry association VIA Nederland, 74 per cent of spend on online display ads goes to variants based on personal data. The data for these personalised ads often come from different sources; from visited websites and downloaded apps to search terms used and videos viewed. Various parties in the market consolidate these data and infer a complete profile from them; from age and place of residence to interests and buying behaviour.

id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0019
text: Personalised ads can neatly match the needs of the website visitor, but according to various studies the drawbacks outweigh the benefits for many people. Terms such as “manipulative”, “invasive” or even “creepy” came up when people were [surveyed](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-658-24878-9_5 "Consumer View on Personalized Advertising: Overview of Self-Reported Benefits and Concerns") for a scientific study about this type of advertising.

metadata.chunk_id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0019
metadata.source_slug: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving
metadata.source_title: Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Leisure, Digitalisering, Wet- en regelgeving
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: This article explains how the Digital Services Act will make ‘tracking’ individuals for advertising purposes more difficult and lists alternatives.
metadata.parent_text: Personalised ads can neatly match the needs of the website visitor, but according to various studies the drawbacks outweigh the benefits for many people. Terms such as “manipulative”, “invasive” or even “creepy” came up when people were [surveyed](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-658-24878-9_5 "Consumer View on Personalized Advertising: Overview of Self-Reported Benefits and Concerns") for a scientific study about this type of advertising. According to [research](https://www.amnesty.org/en/latest/news/2019/12/big-tech-privacy-poll-shows-people-worried/ "New poll reveals 7 in 10 people want governments to regulate Big Tech over personal data fears") by Amnesty International, 77 per cent see profiling people for online advertising purposes as a problem. More than a third of respondents (37 per cent) even avoid certain search terms or web pages for fear of being tracked. At the same time, personalised ads are more effective than fully generic ones; the chance that someone clicks on an advert tailored to online behaviour turns out to be more than [five times as high](https://datadrivenadvertising.eu/wp-content/uploads/2017/09/BehaviouralTargeting_FINAL.pdf "THE ECONOMIC VALUE OF BEHAVIOURAL TARGETING IN DIGITAL ADVERTISING") as with general ads. Advertisers are therefore willing to pay more for this variant. When Apple made a technical change to the Safari web browser, meaning users could no longer be tracked by default for personalised ads, the ad price fell by [60 per cent](https://www.theinformation.com/articles/inside-apples-decision-to-blow-up-the-digital-ads-business "Inside Apple’s Decision to Blow Up the Digital Ads Business"). Tracking internet users will be made more difficult. The Digital Services Act does not ban tracking. However, the new legislation makes it harder to track internet users for advertising purposes. That is primarily due to specific restrictions. As part of a broader set of measures to protect young people on online platforms, minors may no longer be shown personalised ads at all. For adults, this is in principle allowed, as long as the personalisation is not based on sensitive data. This includes, for example, information about health, sexual orientation, religion or political opinions.

At the same time, personalised ads are more effective than fully generic ones; the chance that someone clicks on an advert tailored to online behaviour turns out to be more than [five times as high](https://datadrivenadvertising.eu/wp-content/uploads/2017/09/BehaviouralTargeting_FINAL.pdf "THE ECONOMIC VALUE OF BEHAVIOURAL TARGETING IN DIGITAL ADVERTISING") as with general ads. Advertisers are therefore willing to pay more for this variant. When Apple made a technical change to the Safari web browser, meaning users could no longer be tracked by default for personalised ads, the ad price fell by [60 per cent](https://www.theinformation.com/articles/inside-apples-decision-to-blow-up-the-digital-ads-business "Inside Apple’s Decision to Blow Up the Digital Ads Business"). Tracking internet users will be made more difficult. The Digital Services Act does not ban tracking. However, the new legislation makes it harder to track internet users for advertising purposes. That is primarily due to specific restrictions. As part of a broader set of measures to protect young people on online platforms, minors may no longer be shown personalised ads at all. For adults, this is in principle allowed, as long as the personalisation is not based on sensitive data. This includes, for example, information about health, sexual orientation, religion or political opinions.

id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0053
text: The new rules on tracking in the DSA apply to ‘very large online platforms’, with a reach of more than 45 million users in the European Union, and ‘online platforms’. The ‘very large online platforms’ category includes the channels of international tech giants, led by the ‘duopoly’ Meta – the parent company of Instagram and Facebook – and Alphabet, the company behind search giant Google.

metadata.parent_text: The new rules on tracking in the DSA apply to ‘very large online platforms’, with a reach of more than 45 million users in the European Union, and ‘online platforms’. The ‘very large online platforms’ category includes the channels of international tech giants, led by the ‘duopoly’ Meta – the parent company of Instagram and Facebook – and Alphabet, the company behind search giant Google. It is not surprising that the DSA regulates the large platform companies; together they pocket the majority of global digital advertising revenues.

metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: This article explains how the Digital Services Act will make ‘tracking’ individuals for advertising purposes more difficult and lists alternatives.
metadata.parent_text: Part of their revenue stream comes from the ad space they offer to advertisers for a fee. In the Google ecosystem, this could be a YouTube advert or a listing among the search engine’s ‘sponsored results’. On Instagram and Facebook, these are the ads that users see when they scroll through their feed. Because of the large amount of data these big tech companies collect about their users, they can easily give advertisers access to very specific target groups. The other revenue stream derives from their role as intermediaries; many companies rely on Google AdSense – and to a lesser extent the Facebook Audience Network – to fill the ad space on their own website or mobile app. These ads are in many cases personalised based on behaviours on, and data shared with, the platforms in question. It is therefore evident that restrictions on tracking pose a threat to the ad-based business model of Meta and Alphabet. This is also evident from the fact that both companies actively lobbied when the initial proposal spoke of a total ban on personalised ads.

text: However, this does not mean that companies without an explicit platform business model can sit back. The new rules will therefore probably also require vigilance from, for example, news websites and online retailers. Baneke does stress that it is difficult to give a definitive answer at this stage. “Because the law has not yet entered into force, there is also no case law in which the term ‘online platform’ is further specified.”. The scope of the European law is therefore broad.

Baneke does stress that it is difficult to give a definitive answer at this stage. “Because the law has not yet entered into force, there is also no case law in which the term ‘online platform’ is further specified.”. The scope of the European law is therefore broad.

The scope of the European law is therefore broad.

text: Although it is not yet possible to determine exactly how many companies in the Netherlands will be affected, it is already clear that many websites depend on advertising revenue. Of the ten million most-visited websites worldwide, almost [47 per cent](https://w3techs.com/technologies/history_overview/advertising/all "Historical trends in the usage statistics of advertising networks for websites") use an ‘ad network’.

metadata.parent_text: Although it is not yet possible to determine exactly how many companies in the Netherlands will be affected, it is already clear that many websites depend on advertising revenue. Of the ten million most-visited websites worldwide, almost [47 per cent](https://w3techs.com/technologies/history_overview/advertising/all "Historical trends in the usage statistics of advertising networks for websites") use an ‘ad network’. This is a technological solution that aggregates online ad space from publishers and offers it to advertisers. The extent to which these websites will still be able to generate revenue through personalised advertising is likely to decline sharply. The new process requirements make it easier for internet users to refuse tracking. The consequences of redesigning such a process should not be underestimated.

id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0089
text: For advertisers who rely on personalised ads and publishers who depend on the revenues from such ads, the partial loss of this option may feel unsettling. However, there are also effective advertising strategies that do not depend on tracking individual internet users.

metadata.chunk_id: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0089
metadata.source_slug: Advertentiemodel onder druk door nieuwe Europese wetgeving
metadata.source_title: Advertising model under pressure due to new European legislation
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, Retail, Leisure, Digitalisering, Wet- en regelgeving
metadata.author: Julia Krauwer
metadata.description: This article explains how the Digital Services Act will make ‘tracking’ individuals for advertising purposes more difficult and lists alternatives.
metadata.parent_text: For advertisers who rely on personalised ads and publishers who depend on the revenues from such ads, the partial loss of this option may feel unsettling. However, there are also effective advertising strategies that do not depend on tracking individual internet users. For example, due to the clear preference of its website visitors, the NPO switched entirely to ‘contextual advertising’ in 2020. The person themselves is not decisive, but rather the type of website, article or video being viewed determines the ad that is placed. This allows advertisers to infer the viewer’s interests, demographics and needs without using data about that specific person. Examples of contextual advertising include a lipstick advert on a beauty blog, or an offer for a family pack of ice cream alongside a news item about hot weather. This strategy proved beneficial for both broadcaster and advertisers; advertisers saw the effectiveness of their campaigns [remain the same or even increase](https://www.ster.nl/media/quakpy4e/ster_een-toekomst-zonder-advertentiecookies.pdf "EEN TOEKOMST ZONDER ADVERTENTIECOOKIES?"), and the NPO saw online advertising revenues rise. In [De Volkskrant](https://www.volkskrant.nl/wetenschap/pop-ups-voor-toestemming-cookies-illegaal-bevonden-je-kunt-niet-vragen-om-akkoord-te-gaan-met-een-datalek~b578a408/ "Pop-ups voor toestemming cookies illegaal bevonden: ‘Je kunt niet vragen om akkoord te gaan met een datalek’"), Joost Negenman, a privacy officer at the broadcaster, said earlier this year that while the number of ads placed has gone down, revenue per ad has increased because the NPO stopped using Google as an intermediary for ad placement. Instead, the NPO started dealing directly with advertisers. “An enormous amount of money gets stuck with intermediaries in the entire chain,” Negenman said in the newspaper. “If you make a direct link between the advertiser and the platform, almost all the amount the advertiser spends can also benefit the platform.” According to Google, the share of ad revenues it retains is around [thirty per cent](https://blog.google/products/admanager/display-buying-share-revenue-publishers/ "How our display buying platforms share revenue with publishers"). By placing ads smartly in context and doing business directly with advertisers, the NPO essentially reverted to an old advertising model from the offline world: the advertising model of printed newspapers. Newspapers, platforms avant la lettre, have been selling ads for hundreds of years to advertisers who in turn want to reach the newspaper’s readers. The NPO’s experience shows that this old advertising model can work well through the use of contextual advertising. There are also alternatives that still try to target people, but use far less granular and privacy-sensitive data to do so.

In [De Volkskrant](https://www.volkskrant.nl/wetenschap/pop-ups-voor-toestemming-cookies-illegaal-bevonden-je-kunt-niet-vragen-om-akkoord-te-gaan-met-een-datalek~b578a408/ "Pop-ups voor toestemming cookies illegaal bevonden: ‘Je kunt niet vragen om akkoord te gaan met een datalek’"), Joost Negenman, a privacy officer at the broadcaster, said earlier this year that while the number of ads placed has gone down, revenue per ad has increased because the NPO stopped using Google as an intermediary for ad placement. Instead, the NPO started dealing directly with advertisers. “An enormous amount of money gets stuck with intermediaries in the entire chain,” Negenman said in the newspaper. “If you make a direct link between the advertiser and the platform, almost all the amount the advertiser spends can also benefit the platform.” According to Google, the share of ad revenues it retains is around [thirty per cent](https://blog.google/products/admanager/display-buying-share-revenue-publishers/ "How our display buying platforms share revenue with publishers"). By placing ads smartly in context and doing business directly with advertisers, the NPO essentially reverted to an old advertising model from the offline world: the advertising model of printed newspapers. Newspapers, platforms avant la lettre, have been selling ads for hundreds of years to advertisers who in turn want to reach the newspaper’s readers. The NPO’s experience shows that this old advertising model can work well through the use of contextual advertising. There are also alternatives that still try to target people, but use far less granular and privacy-sensitive data to do so.
